annotations.label: Magic square

anchor.snippet: a magic square is a square array of numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of magic squares; grep for 'magic' finds only unrelated identifiers.

annotations.label: Order and magic constant

anchor.snippet: The order of a magic square is the number of integers along one side

annotations.note: No magic-square order/constant concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal magic square

anchor.snippet: the magic square is said to be normal

annotations.note: Normal magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trivial magic square

anchor.snippet: Magic squares that include repeated entries do not fall under this definition

annotations.note: No magic-square definition exists, so this distinction is absent.

annotations.label: Semimagic square

anchor.snippet: When all the rows and columns but not both diagonals sum to the magic constant

annotations.note: Semimagic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classification by order

anchor.snippet: Magic squares are generally classified according to their order n as

annotations.note: No magic-square order classification exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Square of Varahamihira

anchor.snippet: The square of Varahamihira as given above has sum of 18

annotations.note: This specific historical magic square is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nagarjuniya square

anchor.snippet: The Nagarjuniya square is a pan-diagonal magic square

annotations.note: This specific magic square is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lo Shu uniqueness

anchor.snippet: is the unique normal magic square of order three in which 1 is at the bottom and 2 is in the upper right corner

annotations.note: No formalization of order-3 magic square uniqueness exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order-3 squares from Lo Shu

anchor.snippet: Every normal magic square of order three is obtained from the Lo Shu by rotation or reflection

annotations.note: This classification of order-3 squares is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chautisa Yantra

anchor.snippet: This is known as the Chautisa Yantra

annotations.note: This specific magic square is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dürer's magic square

anchor.snippet: The order four normal magic square Albrecht Dürer immortalized

annotations.note: Dürer's magic square is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sagrada Família magic square

anchor.snippet: features a trivial order 4 magic square

annotations.note: This specific magic square is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Magic constant formula

anchor.snippet: Every normal magic square has a constant dependent on the order n

annotations.note: The magic constant formula n(n²+1)/2 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Magic constants for small orders

anchor.snippet: For normal magic squares of orders n = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8, the magic constants are, respectively

annotations.note: These specific magic-constant values are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trivial order-1 square

anchor.section: Magic square of order 1 is trivial
anchor.snippet: The 1×1 magic square, with only one cell containing the number 1, is called trivial

annotations.note: No magic-square definition exists in Mathlib for this case.

annotations.label: No order-2 magic square

anchor.section: Magic square of order 2 cannot be constructed
anchor.snippet: Normal magic squares of all sizes can be constructed except 2×2

annotations.note: Nonexistence of the 2×2 magic square is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Center of mass

anchor.snippet: the center of mass of a magic square coincides with its geometric center

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general Finset.centerMass but no magic-square center-of-mass result.

annotations.label: Moment of inertia

anchor.snippet: The moment of inertia of a magic square has been defined as the sum over all cells

annotations.note: This magic-square moment-of-inertia definition is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equal moment of inertia

anchor.snippet: all magic squares of a given order have the same moment of inertia as each other

annotations.note: This result about magic squares is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Birkhoff–von Neumann theorem

anchor.snippet: The Birkhoff–von Neumann theorem states that for any doubly stochastic matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the set of doubly stochastic matrices is the convex hull of permutation matrices, i.e. each is a convex combination of permutation matrices.

annotations.label: Marcus-Ree theorem

anchor.snippet: By Marcus-Ree theorem, however, there need not be more than

annotations.note: The Marcus-Ree bound on the number of permutation matrices is not in Mathlib's Birkhoff file or elsewhere.

annotations.label: Semi-magic square

anchor.snippet: Semi-magic square when its rows and columns sum to give the magic constant

annotations.note: Semi-magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple magic square

anchor.snippet: Simple magic square when its rows, columns, and two diagonals sum to give magic constant and no more

annotations.note: Simple magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Self-complementary magic square

anchor.snippet: Self-complementary magic square when it is a magic square which when complemented

annotations.note: Self-complementary magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Associative magic square

anchor.snippet: Associative magic square when it is a magic square with a further property that every number added to the number equidistant

annotations.note: Associative magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pandiagonal magic square

anchor.snippet: Pandiagonal magic square when it is a magic square with a further property that the broken diagonals sum to the magic constant

annotations.note: Pandiagonal magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ultra magic square

anchor.snippet: Ultra magic square when it is both associative and pandiagonal magic square

annotations.note: Ultra magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bordered magic square

anchor.snippet: Bordered magic square when it is a magic square and it remains magic when the rows and columns on the outer edge are removed

annotations.note: Bordered magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Composite magic square

anchor.snippet: Composite magic square when it is a magic square that is created by

annotations.note: Composite magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inlaid magic square

anchor.snippet: Inlaid magic square when it is a magic square inside which a magic sub-square is embedded

annotations.note: Inlaid magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Most-perfect magic square

anchor.snippet: Most-perfect magic square when it is a pandiagonal magic square with two further properties

annotations.note: Most-perfect magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Franklin magic square

anchor.snippet: Franklin magic square when it is a doubly even magic square with three further properties

annotations.note: Franklin magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multimagic square

anchor.snippet: Multimagic square when it is a magic square that remains magic even if all its numbers are replaced by their

annotations.note: Multimagic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Single order-3 equivalence class

anchor.snippet: the set of normal squares of order three constitutes a single equivalence class

annotations.note: This enumeration fact about order-3 squares is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Count of magic squares orders 1-6

anchor.snippet: The number of different n × n magic squares for n from 1 to 6, not counting rotations and reflections is:

annotations.note: These enumeration counts are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Count of magic tori orders 1-5

anchor.snippet: The number of magic tori of order n from 1 to 5, is:

annotations.note: Magic tori counts are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invariance under multiplication

anchor.snippet: A magic square remains magic when its numbers are multiplied by any constant

annotations.note: No magic-square invariance results exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Invariance under addition/complement

anchor.snippet: A magic square remains magic when a constant is added or subtracted to its numbers

annotations.note: No magic-square invariance results exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Substitution by arithmetic progressions

anchor.snippet: The numbers of a magic square can be substituted with corresponding numbers from a set of s arithmetic progressions

annotations.note: This magic-square construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eight equivalent squares

anchor.snippet: Any magic square can be rotated and reflected to produce 8 trivially distinct squares

annotations.note: The dihedral symmetry of magic squares is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diagonal cell row/column interchange

anchor.snippet: interchanging the row and the column which intersect in a cell on a diagonal

annotations.note: This magic-square transformation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Same-side row interchange

anchor.snippet: interchanging two rows on one side of the center line

annotations.note: This magic-square transformation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-central row/complement interchange

anchor.snippet: A magic square remains magic when any of its non-central rows x and y are interchanged

annotations.note: This magic-square transformation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diagonal quadrant interchange

anchor.snippet: A magic square remains magic when its quadrants are diagonally interchanged

annotations.note: This magic-square transformation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Associative equidistant interchange

anchor.snippet: An associative magic square remains associative when two rows or columns equidistant from the center are interchanged

annotations.note: Associative magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Associative same-sided interchange

anchor.snippet: remains associative when two same sided rows (or columns) are interchanged

annotations.note: Associative magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pandiagonal cyclic shift

anchor.snippet: A pan-diagonal magic square remains a pan-diagonal magic square under cyclic shifting of rows or of columns or both

annotations.note: Pandiagonal magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bordered border permutation

anchor.snippet: A bordered magic square remains a bordered magic square after permuting the border cells in the rows or columns

annotations.note: Bordered magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bordered border rotation/reflection

anchor.snippet: A bordered magic square remains a bordered magic square after each of its concentric borders are independently rotated or reflected

annotations.note: Bordered magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Composite sub-square transforms

anchor.snippet: A composite magic square remains a composite magic square when the embedded magic squares undergo transformations

annotations.note: Composite magic square transformations are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence for all orders except 2

anchor.snippet: Magic squares are believed to exist for all orders, except for order 2

annotations.note: Existence of magic squares for all orders is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bimagic/trimagic/multimagic

anchor.snippet: If raising each number to the n th power yields another magic square

annotations.note: Bimagic/trimagic/multimagic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Alphamagic square

anchor.snippet: the number of letters in the name of each number in the square generates another magic square

annotations.note: Alphamagic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Magic squares of primes

anchor.snippet: Rudolf Ondrejka (1928–2001) discovered the following 3×3 magic square of primes

annotations.note: This prime magic square example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Green–Tao implies large prime magic squares

anchor.snippet: The Green–Tao theorem implies that there are arbitrarily large magic squares consisting of primes

annotations.note: The Green–Tao theorem appears in docs/1000.yaml only as an unformalized target (no decl), so neither it nor this corollary is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversible magic square

anchor.snippet: has a magic constant of 264 both upside down and right way up

annotations.note: This reversible magic square example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Birthday magic square

anchor.snippet: such magic squares are called birthday magic square

annotations.note: Birthday magic squares are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiplicative magic square

anchor.snippet: a multiplicative magic square has a constant product of numbers

annotations.note: Multiplicative magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiplicative from additive

anchor.snippet: A multiplicative magic square can be derived from an additive magic square by raising 2

annotations.note: This construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex multiplicative magic squares

anchor.section: Multiplicative magic squares of complex numbers
anchor.snippet: it is possible to produce an infinity of multiplicative magic squares of complex numbers

annotations.note: Complex multiplicative magic squares are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Additive-multiplicative squares

anchor.snippet: Additive-multiplicative magic squares and semimagic squares satisfy properties of both

annotations.note: Additive-multiplicative squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nonexistence of small additive-multiplicative squares

anchor.snippet: it has been proven that no 3×3 or 4×4 additive-multiplicative magic squares

annotations.note: This nonexistence result is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geometric magic square

anchor.snippet: Such squares, known as geometric magic squares , were invented and named by Lee Sallows in 2001

annotations.note: Geometric magic squares are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numerical as 1D geometric

anchor.snippet: numerical magic squares are that special case of a geometric magic square using one dimensional shapes

annotations.note: This relationship is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear area magic square

anchor.snippet: the first linear area magic square (L-AMS) was constructed by Walter Trump

annotations.note: Area magic squares are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General magic design

anchor.snippet: The general case is to consider a design with N parts to be magic if the N parts are labeled with the numbers 1 through N

annotations.note: This general magic-design notion is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constraint on magic shapes

anchor.snippet: Possible magic shapes are constrained by the number of equal-sized, equal-sum subsets

annotations.note: This constraint is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Magic squares to n-queens

anchor.snippet: published a method for converting some magic squares into n -queens solutions

annotations.note: Neither magic squares nor an n-queens conversion method exists in Mathlib.
